summary: Dan Shapiro introduces a multi-part blog series documenting how he built a custom coffee table with a slate inset. He shares the blueprints for the project under a Creative Commons license, allowing others to build from his plans but not modify or sell them commercially. The post is largely an introduction and table of contents for the series, with self-deprecating humor about his technical drawings. He notes the plans were created in TurboCAD v8 and explains his licensing rationale based on past experiences with people reselling free work.
key_points: Shapiro shares open blueprints for a handmade coffee table with slate inset, his first major furniture project
The plans are released under a Creative Commons license that permits reproduction but prohibits commercial use and modification
He justifies the restrictive licensing by citing experiences with people relabeling and reselling free work
The series spans four parts covering design, construction, assembly, and finishing
money_quotes: The profs who taught me proper GD&T will kick my ass when they see it.
Still, it was good enough to print out, scatter copies of around the shop, spill shellac on, and ultimately produce a table from.
I've seen too many people have their free work relabeled and resold for me to act otherwise.
key_insight: Shapiro's early maker ethos is evident in openly sharing furniture blueprints while carefully protecting them with Creative Commons licensing — balancing generosity with hard-won lessons about free work being exploited.
